cards.q: In what environment do spores of fungi occur?
cards.a: In the hot and humid, or arid, tropical environment.

cards.q: What factors favour the spread of fungal infections among children?
cards.a: Poor personal and community hygiene; closeness among large families; and institutions such as schools, camps and barracks.

cards.q: Which body structures do most fungal infections affect?
cards.a: The skin, hair, nails and mucosa.

cards.q: List the five superficial fungal skin infections.
cards.a: Candidiasis, tinea capitis, tinea corporis, tinea pedis, and pityriasis versicolor.

cards.q: List the five deep fungal skin infections.
cards.a: Mycetoma (Madura foot), blastomycosis, coccidiomycosis, histoplasmosis, and sporotrichosis.

cards.q: Which organisms cause candidiasis?
cards.a: Candida albicans, C. tropicalis, C. stellatoidea, C. krusei, or other yeasts of the genus Candida.

cards.q: Where do candida infections usually occur?
cards.a: On the skin, mucous membranes, nail folds and nails, gastrointestinal tract and genital tract; in systemic infections the viscera may be involved.

cards.q: Describe the skin lesions of candidiasis and where they occur.
cards.a: Dusky red macerated or erosive lesions in the skin folds of the neck, flanks, large flexures and limbs.

cards.q: What may follow scratching of candidal skin lesions?
cards.a: Fissuring and bleeding, with occurrence of secondary bacterial infection.

cards.q: Which organism most commonly causes oral thrush?
cards.a: Candida albicans.

cards.q: Describe the lesions of oral candidiasis.
cards.a: Curdy or whitish-grey, friable, cheesy pseudomembranous patches or plaques on markedly reddened mucosa.

cards.q: Where may the plaques of thrush be found?
cards.a: On the lips, buccal mucosa, tongue and palate, extending to the oesophagus/pharynx — they invade the mucosa superficially.

cards.q: What happens on removal of the plaques of thrush, and why is this useful?
cards.a: It may cause mild punctate areas of bleeding, which helps to confirm the diagnosis.

cards.q: List the factors predisposing to candidiasis.
cards.a: Diabetes mellitus, retroviral disease, prolonged antibiotic and corticosteroid use, and leukaemia.

cards.q: Give the treatment of oral candidiasis.
cards.a: Antifungal drugs such as clotrimazole, nystatin, amphotericin B or miconazole gel; and proper oral hygiene.

cards.q: What is tinea capitis and which organism most often causes it?
cards.a: A dermatophyte infection of the scalp, most often caused by Trichophyton tonsurans; occasionally by Microsporum canis, and much less commonly by other Microsporum and Trichophyton species.

cards.q: Describe the lesions and hairs in tinea capitis.
cards.a: Erythematous and scaly circular plaques (ringworm), within which the infected hairs become brittle and broken with different lengths.

cards.q: What appearance do multiple affected sites produce in tinea capitis?
cards.a: A moth-eaten appearance of the hair.

cards.q: What else may develop in tinea capitis?
cards.a: Numerous confluent patches of alopecia; scaling and inflammation of the scalp are variable, and patients may complain of severe pruritus.

cards.q: What is a kerion and what causes it?
cards.a: An inflammatory and infected boggy mass covered in crusts, pus-filled bumps and localized hair loss — caused by the host's cellular immune response to tinea capitis.

cards.q: What is favus and which organism causes it?
cards.a: A severe, chronic form of tinea capitis, typically caused by the dermatophyte Trichophyton schoenleinii.

cards.q: Describe the lesions of favus.
cards.a: Thick, yellowish, cup-shaped yellow crusts called scutula that develop around hair follicles, often accompanied by a distinctive mousy odour; also termed honey-combed scalp lesions with yellow crusts and scales.

cards.q: What happens if favus is left untreated?
cards.a: It destroys the hair follicles, causes matting of the hair, and causes permanent scarring hair loss (alopecia).

cards.q: How is tinea capitis diagnosed and treated?
cards.a: Diagnosis by scraping and KOH examination; treatment with oral griseofulvin, itraconazole or terbinafine, plus topical antifungal cream or shampoo.

cards.q: Define tinea corporis.
cards.a: A fungal infection of the glabrous skin, excluding the palms, soles and groin.

cards.q: Which organisms most commonly cause tinea corporis?
cards.a: Trichophyton rubrum and Trichophyton mentagrophytes; infections with Microsporum canis are also frequent.

cards.q: Describe the characteristic lesion of tinea corporis.
cards.a: A dry, mildly erythematous, elevated, scaly papule or plaque that spreads centrifugally and clears centrally to form the characteristic annular lesion; the active edge shows vesicles and pustules.

cards.q: How is tinea corporis acquired?
cards.a: Usually by direct contact from an infected person, surfaces, and pets (M. canis).

cards.q: Name the forms of tinea corporis according to site.
cards.a: Tinea faciale (face), tinea axillaris (axilla), tinea cruris (groin), tinea manuum (hands), and tinea unguium (nails).

cards.q: How is tinea corporis diagnosed?
cards.a: Clinically; by skin scraping and KOH examination; and by culture of skin scrapings for identifying the species of dermatophyte.

cards.q: Give the treatment of tinea corporis.
cards.a: Topical antifungal agents such as imidazoles, terbinafine or naftifine; and in severe cases oral griseofulvin or itraconazole.

cards.q: What is tinea pedis and which organisms cause it?
cards.a: Athlete's foot — a fungal infection of the toe webs and soles of the feet, caused by Trichophyton rubrum, Trichophyton mentagrophytes and Epidermophyton floccosum.

cards.q: Describe the usual appearance of tinea pedis.
cards.a: The subdigital crevices are fissured, with maceration and peeling of the surrounding skin.

cards.q: Describe the less common presentations of tinea pedis.
cards.a: Less commonly, a chronic diffuse hyperkeratosis of the sole of the foot occurs with only mild erythema; occasionally the papules progress to vesicles and bullae that may become pustular.

cards.q: Which symptoms are characteristic of tinea pedis?
cards.a: Severe tenderness, itching, and a persistent foul odour.

cards.q: How is tinea pedis diagnosed and treated?
cards.a: Diagnosis by fungal mycelia seen on microscopic examination of a KOH preparation, or by culture. Treatment: avoidance of occlusive footwear, careful drying between the toes after bathing, topical zinc undecylenate powder, imidazole cream, and oral griseofulvin.

cards.q: What is pityriasis versicolor and which organism causes it?
cards.a: A common chronic fungal infection of the stratum corneum, caused by the dimorphic yeast Malassezia furfur, found in areas of skin rich in sebum production.

cards.q: In whom is pityriasis versicolor most prevalent?
cards.a: Adolescents and young adults.

cards.q: List the predisposing factors to pityriasis versicolor.
cards.a: A warm humid environment, excessive sweating, occlusion, high plasma cortisol levels, immunosuppression, malnourishment, and genetically determined susceptibility.

cards.q: Describe the lesions of pityriasis versicolor and their sites.
cards.a: Hypopigmented or hyperpigmented macules covered with a fine scale, often merging to form confluent patches; most commonly on the neck, upper chest, back and upper arms.

cards.q: How is pityriasis versicolor diagnosed?
cards.a: Wood lamp, KOH preparation of scrapings, and skin biopsy.

cards.q: Give the treatment of pityriasis versicolor.
cards.a: Selenium sulfide suspension applied overnight; imidazole or terbinafine cream twice daily for 2–4 weeks; and oral therapy with ketoconazole, fluconazole or itraconazole.

cards.q: What causes histoplasmosis and how is it acquired?
cards.a: Histoplasma capsulatum and H. duboisii — dimorphic fungi acquired by inhalation of fungal spores.

cards.q: Where are the fungi of histoplasmosis found?
cards.a: In the soil and in the droppings of some animals, e.g. bats and poultry.

cards.q: Describe the spread of histoplasmosis.
cards.a: The primary infection is in the lungs, with haematogenous spread to the liver, bone marrow, spleen, lymph nodes and subcutaneous tissue.

cards.q: How does African histoplasmosis (H. duboisii) present?
cards.a: As subcutaneous nodules which tend to umbilicate and suppurate or ulcerate as they grow.

cards.q: How does H. capsulatum characteristically present?
cards.a: It affects the lungs, and may present with productive cough and constitutional symptoms.

cards.q: How is histoplasmosis diagnosed and treated?
cards.a: By isolation and identification of the causative fungus in cultures from sputum, bone marrow, lymph node biopsy, or pus or scrapings from ulcers. Treatment: IV amphotericin B; oral itraconazole or ketoconazole.

cards.q: What causes sporotrichosis and how is it acquired?
cards.a: Accidental implantation into the skin of Sporothrix schenckii, which grows on thorny plants.

cards.q: Describe the initial lesion of sporotrichosis.
cards.a: A firm, painless or mildly tender subcutaneous nodule at the site of skin trauma, presenting as a purple, violaceous, hyperpigmented brown, or dark grey nodule.

cards.q: How does the sporotrichosis nodule progress?
cards.a: The primary nodule grows larger, becomes pustular, and may open into a chronic, hollowed-out sore (ulceration).

cards.q: How may sporotrichosis spread?
cards.a: By lymphatic or haematogenous routes to muscles, bones, joints or other tissues; inhalation of spores may cause lung infection.

cards.q: How is sporotrichosis diagnosed and treated?
cards.a: Diagnosis by microscopic demonstration of the fungus in preparations from scrapings or biopsy. Treatment: potassium iodide, IV amphotericin B, or oral itraconazole.

cards.q: What causes blastomycosis and how is it acquired?
cards.a: Blastomyces dermatitidis, acquired by inhalation of fungal spores.

cards.q: Describe the spread and skin lesions of blastomycosis.
cards.a: Primary infection is in the lungs with haematogenous spread to the skin, bone and genital tract; it presents as irregularly crusted plaques, superficial ulcerative lesions, or firm subcutaneous nodules on exposed parts of the body.

cards.q: How is blastomycosis diagnosed and treated?
cards.a: Diagnosis by characteristic budding cells seen in KOH preparation of materials from the lesion; treatment with IV amphotericin B.

cards.q: What causes coccidiomycosis and how does it spread?
cards.a: Coccidioides immitis, a dimorphic fungus acquired by inhalation of fungal spores; primary infection is in the lungs with haematogenous spread to the skin, subcutaneous tissue and bones.

cards.q: What do the skin lesions of coccidiomycosis consist of, and how is it treated?
cards.a: Panniculitis, abscesses and draining sinuses; treated with oral ketoconazole or itraconazole.

cards.q: What is mycetoma and which structures does it affect?
cards.a: Madura foot — a chronic infection of the skin, subcutaneous tissue and bones of the feet.

cards.q: How does mycetoma develop, and in whom is it common?
cards.a: It takes a very long time to develop and is rare in children; the microorganisms are typically introduced through minor injuries like a splinter or thorn prick, which is why it is common among agricultural workers.

cards.q: Distinguish eumycetoma from actinomycetoma.
cards.a: Eumycetoma is caused by fungi; actinomycetoma is caused by filamentous bacteria.

cards.q: Name the causative organisms of mycetoma.
cards.a: Actinomycetes such as Nocardia brasiliensis, Actinomadura madurae and Streptomyces somaliensis; and fungi such as Pseudallescheria boydii, Madurella grisea or Madurella mycetomatis.

cards.q: What is the treatment of mycetoma?
cards.a: Itraconazole.
